content: Let me read the necessary files to complete this blueprint review task. I'll start with the instructions and context files, then read the specific chapter files mentioned in the directive.
I'll read the instructions and context files first, then audit the three chapters.

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan, dag]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Dependency-graph integrity (use `leandag`)** — the `\uses{}` edges must reflect the real mathematical dependencies. `leandag` computes the actual DAG, so use it instead of eyeballing:
130	  - **Broken edges** — `leandag build --json` reports `unknown_uses` (a `\uses{}` pointing at a label no declaration defines). Every one is a must-fix.
131	  - **Missing edges** — read each proof: if it relies on a lemma/definition that its `\uses{}` does not list, the edge is missing. These don't show up as "broken"; you find them by reading the math against the declared `\uses{}`. Missing edges make the loop dispatch provers out of order, so flag them as correctness findings.
132	  - **Isolated declarations** — `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated --type theorem`, and the `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists nodes with **no `\uses{}` out and nothing using them**. An isolated *blueprint* node is almost always a *symptom of a missing edge*, not dead weight — so for each one decide and record a disposition (see below). Isolated `lean_aux` nodes are uncovered Lean helpers, a separate "needs a blueprint entry" signal, not removal candidates.
133	- **Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)** — it is good practice for a chapter that relies on a Mathlib-provided result to state it as an explicit anchor block marked `\mathlibok` (with `\lean{}` naming the real Mathlib declaration), rather than a bare `\uses{}` to nothing. Treat such anchors as *valid and done* — never flag them as ∞ holes or `remove` candidates. But **audit each `\mathlibok` claim for faithfulness**, because a wrong one makes the loop skip a real gap: does Mathlib actually contain the named `\lean{}` declaration, and does the stated form match it? Verify against Lean (`archon-lean-lsp`) when in doubt. A `\mathlibok` on a statement Mathlib does not provide — or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's — is a hard fail (treat like a fabricated citation). Also: where a broken `\uses{}` points at what is really a Mathlib result, the right `wire-up` is "add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it," not "invent a project lemma."
134	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
135	- **Rendering integrity (run `archon blueprint-doctor`)** — the blueprint must *render*: as the compiled leanblueprint site and in the dashboard. The doctor lints this deterministically — run `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once and triage every `malformed_refs` finding into your report:
136	  - **`undefined-macro`** — a chapter uses `\foo` that no `macros/*.tex` or chapter-local `\providecommand` defines: it renders as raw TeX everywhere. Recommend the definition (usually obvious from context: `\Z` → `\mathbb{Z}`, `\fppf` → `\mathrm{fppf}`); when the intent is genuinely unclear (you cannot tell what the writer meant), flag it as a correctness finding for the chapter's writer instead of guessing.
137	  - **`math-delim`** — interleaved `$ … \( … \) … $` delimiters that shred formulas mid-sentence; per-line locations are in the finding. Must-fix, writer-directive material.
138	  - **`literal-ref` / `bare-label`** — placeholder "REF" tokens and raw label ids in prose; each needs a `\cref{}` or the human-readable number.
139	  Group these per chapter in your findings so the dispatcher can scope one writer per damaged chapter.
140	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
141	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
142	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
143	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
144	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
145	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
146	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
147	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
148	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
149	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
150	
151	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
152	
153	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
154	
155	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
156	
157	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
158	
159	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
160	
161	## Always read everything
162	
163	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
164	
165	## Unstarted-phase proposals
166	
167	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
168	
169	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. (That table lists only *remaining* phases — finished ones live in `## Completed` and need no proposal.) For each remaining phase row whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
170	
171	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
172	
173	The proposal answers:
174	
175	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
176	
177	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
178	
179	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
180	
181	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
182	
183	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
184	
185	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
186	
187	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
188	
189	```
190	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
191	
192	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
193	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
194	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
195	
196	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
197	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
198	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
199	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
200	...
201	
202	**`\uses` skeleton**:
203	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
204	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
205	
206	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
207	
208	**References for writer**:
209	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
210	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
211	
212	**Subphase choices exposed**:
213	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
214	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
215	```
216	
217	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
218	
219	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
220	
221	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
222	
223	## Directive Format
224	
225	```markdown
226	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
227	
228	## Slug
229	<slug>
230	
231	## Strategy snapshot
232	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
233	
234	## Routes
235	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
236	
237	## References
238	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
239	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
240	
241	## Focus areas (optional)
242	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
243	
244	## Known issues
245	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
246	```
247	
248	## What you do
249	
250	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
251	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
252	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
253	   - Read the entire chapter.
254	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
255	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
256	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
257	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
258	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
259	6. **Audit the dependency graph with `leandag`.** Run `leandag build --json` (broken/unknown `\uses{}`, `unmatched_lean`, isolated count) and `leandag show isolated` once for the whole blueprint. Then triage each isolated **blueprint** node into exactly one disposition and record it:
260	   - **wire-up** — it *should* connect to the graph but an edge is missing or mislabeled. Name the `\uses{}` that should be added (and on which side). This is the common case and a correctness finding — recommend a writer fix, do NOT recommend removal.
261	   - **remove** — it is genuinely orphaned scaffolding: nothing in the goal's proof closure needs it, it isn't the goal, and it isn't a `\mathlibok`/reference anchor. Only then is removal the right call. Name the chapter + label so the plan agent can authorize a writer to delete it.
262	   - **keep** — it is intentionally standalone (the goal theorem with nothing above it, a deliberately isolated `\mathlibok` reference, etc.). Say why and move on.
263	   You **flag and recommend**; you never edit. The plan agent turns a `wire-up`/`remove` disposition into a blueprint-writer directive.
264	7. **Run the rendering lint.** `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once for the whole blueprint; triage every `malformed_refs` finding (`undefined-macro`, `math-delim`, `literal-ref`, `bare-label`) per the "Rendering integrity" bullet above, grouped per chapter.
265	8. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
266	9. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
267	10. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
268	
269	You may also use:
270	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
271	- **`archon blueprint-doctor [--json]`** — the deterministic rendering/structure lint (orphan chapters, broken refs, literal-REF, interleaved math delimiters, bare labels, undefined macros, covers problems). Read-only; run it once per review and triage its `malformed_refs` (see "Rendering integrity" above).
272	- **`leandag`** — your read-only window into the real dependency DAG. Run it freely: it never touches project source or the blueprint. (`leandag build` refreshes the derived `.leandag/` cache, which is regenerated deterministically from the current tree — that is a tool artifact, not a project edit, so it does not violate your read-only rule.)
273	  - `leandag build --json` — the structured build report: `unknown_uses` (broken `\uses{}`), `unmatched_lean` (`\lean{}` pointing nowhere), `conflicts`, and `summary.isolated`.
274	  - `leandag stats` — counts including `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint`.
275	  - `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated [--type theorem] [--chapter <c>]`) — the isolated declarations, which you triage per node (below).
276	  - `leandag show gaps` — blueprint nodes missing a `\lean{}`.
277	
278	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
279	
280	## Report format
281	
282	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md`.
283	
284	**CRITICAL COST RULE**: Your report must be extremely concise to save LLM tokens. Use dense bullet points, abbreviations, and zero conversational filler. DO NOT write paragraphs. Omit any section that has no findings. The plan agent only needs the facts.
285	
286	```markdown
287	# Blueprint Review: <slug>
288	**Iter:** <NNN>
289	
290	## Top-level summaries
291	<OMIT empty sections. Max 1 line per finding.>
292	- **Incomplete**: `Foo.tex` (missing def X); `Bar.tex` (thm:baz proof too thin).
293	- **Bad Lean targets**: `Foo.tex` (\lean{Foo.frob} signature ambiguous).
294	- **Multi-route**: Route A (PARTIAL - `Cohomology.tex`); Route B (MISSING).
295	- **Citations**: `Foo.tex` thm:smooth missing local file parenthetical.
296	- **Deps/Isolated**: `Bar.tex` thm:foo missing `\uses{lem:bar}` (wire-up).
297	
298	## Unstarted-phase proposals
299	<OMIT if all phases covered. Max 3-5 bullets per proposal. NO paragraphs.>
300	### Proposed: `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`
301	- **Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` | **Phase**: <name>
302	- **Key defs**: `def:foo` (`Foo.foo` [expected]), `thm:bar`.
303	- **Skeleton**: `thm:bar` uses `def:foo`.
304	- **Strategy**: <1-2 sentences max>.
305	
306	## Per-chapter
307	<OMIT clean chapters or use 1 line: `Foo.tex: complete/correct`>
308	### `Foo.tex`
309	- **Complete**: false
310	- **Correct**: true
311	- **Notes**: Missing X.
312	
313	## Severity summary
314	<OMIT if clear. Max 1 line per severity tier.>
315	- **must-fix**: `Foo.tex` complete=false.
316	- **soon**: Minor citation issue in `Bar.tex`.
317	```
318	
319	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly.
320	
321	## Return value
322	
323	Your final assistant message:
324	
325	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> chapters audited, <M> findings, <K> unstarted-phase proposals`
326	- Top-level summary counts (incomplete parts, proofs lacking detail, unstarted phases, etc.)
327	- The path to your full report.
328	
329	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
330	
331	## Reminders
332	
333	- **Read every chapter, no scope shortcuts.**
334	- **Cross-reference every strategy phase against existing chapters.** An unstarted phase is not a clean bill of health — it is an opportunity to write the blueprint now and enable parallelism.
335	- **Proposals are actionable, not informational.** Each proposal block must be concrete enough to serve as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Vague "chapter needed for X" notes are not proposals.
336	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
337	- **You audit against the directive's context**, not your own ideas of what the project should look like — but you ARE critical of weak prose and under-specified Lean hints.
338	- **You flag, you don't fix.** Even when the fix is obvious, the plan agent decides what changes next iter.
339	- **The per-chapter shape is fixed**: `complete`, `correct`, `notes`. Don't reshape. The plan agent depends on this format.changes next iter.
340	- **The per-chapter shape is fixed**: `complete`, `correct`, `notes`. Don't reshape. The plan agent depends on this format. this format.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
8	
9	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
10	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
11	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
12	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
13	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
14	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
15	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
16	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
17	single non-zero element of the base ring.
18	
19	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
20	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
21	\(\F\).
22	
23	\section{Generic flatness}
24	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
25	
26	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
27	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
28	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
29	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
30	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
31	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
32	non-zero element of the base ring.
33	
34	\begin{theorem}
35	
36	
37	
38	\leanok
39	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
40	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
41	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
42	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
43	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
44	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
45	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
48	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
49	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
50	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
51	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
52	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
53	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
54	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
55	
56	\end{theorem}
57	
58	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
59	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
60	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
61	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
62	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
63	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
64	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
65	% there exists a finite filtration
66	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
67	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
68	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
69	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
70	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
71	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
72	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
73	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
74	\begin{proof}
75	
76	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
77	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
78	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
79	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
80	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
81	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
82	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
83	
84	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
85	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
86	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
87	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
88	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
89	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
90	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
91	  for exactly this residue.
92	
93	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
94	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
95	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
96	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
97	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
98	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
99	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
100	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
101	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
102	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
103	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
104	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
105	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
106	\end{proof}
107	
108	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
109	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
110	
111	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
112	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
113	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
114	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
115	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
116	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
117	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
118	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
119	
120	\begin{lemma}\leanok
121	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
122	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
124	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
125	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
126	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
127	\end{lemma}
128	
129	\begin{proof}
130	
131	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
132	  finitely presented, so
133	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
134	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
135	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
136	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
137	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
138	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
139	  \(f := r\).
140	\end{proof}
141	
142	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
143	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
144	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
145	  \mathlibok
146	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
147	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
148	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
149	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
150	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
151	\end{lemma}
152	
153	\begin{lemma}\leanok
154	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
155	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
157	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
158	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
159	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
160	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
161	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
162	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
163	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
164	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
165	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
166	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
167	  induction).}
168	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
169	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
170	  \(A_f\).
171	\end{lemma}
172	
173	\begin{proof}
174	
175	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
176	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
177	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
178	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
179	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
180	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
181	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
182	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
183	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
184	\end{proof}

186	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
187	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
188	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
189	  \mathlibok
190	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
191	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
192	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
193	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
194	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
195	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
196	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
197	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
198	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
199	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
200	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
201	\end{lemma}

203	\begin{lemma}\leanok
204	[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
205	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
207	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
208	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
209	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
210	  exact sequence
211	  \[
212	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
213	  \]
214	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
215	\end{lemma}
216	
217	\begin{proof}
218	
219	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
220	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
221	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence.
222	\end{proof}

224	\begin{lemma}\leanok
225	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
226	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
228	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
229	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
230	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{proof}
234	
235	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
236	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
237	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
238	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
239	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
240	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
241	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
242	\end{proof}
243	
244	\begin{lemma}\leanok
245	[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
246	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
248	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
249	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
250	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
251	\end{lemma}
252	
253	\begin{proof}
254	
255	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
256	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
257	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
258	\end{proof}

260	\begin{lemma}\leanok
261	[L3 — splicing fact for a short exact sequence (assembly)]
262	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_shortExact}
264	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
265	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
266	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, splicing remark

268	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
269	  % "Note that if $0\to M'\to M\to M'' \to 0$ is a short exact sequence of
270	  %  $B$-modules, and if $f'$ and $f''$ are non-zero elements of $A$ such that
271	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,
272	  %  then $M_f$ is a free module over $A_f$ where $f=f'f''$. We will use this fact
273	  %  repeatedly. Therefore it is enough to prove the result when $M$ is of the form

276	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (splicing fact).}
277	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
278	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and
279	  \(M''_{f''}\) free over \(A_{f''}\). Then for \(f := f' f''\) the localisation

282	
283	\begin{proof}
284	  \uses{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact,
285	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
286	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1737-L1745).
287	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (splicing fact).}
288	  Set \(f := f' f''\); since \(A\) is a domain and \(f', f'' \ne 0\), the product is
289	  non-zero, so \(f \ne 0\). Localising the given short exact sequence at the powers
290	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence
291	  \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) of \(A_f\)-modules, by
292	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}. Both ends are free over \(A_f\):
293	  applying \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport} to \(M'\) with the
294	  factorisation \(f = f' f''\) (using that \(M'_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\)) shows
295	  \(M'_f\) is free over \(A_f\), and applying the same lemma to \(M''\) with the
296	  factorisation \(f = f'' f'\) (using that \(M''_{f''}\) is free over \(A_{f''}\))
297	  shows \(M''_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Finally
298	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split} concludes that the middle term \(M_f\) is
299	  free over \(A_f\).

302	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
303	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
304	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
305	  \mathlibok
306	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
307	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
308	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
309	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
310	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
311	\end{lemma}
312	
313	\begin{lemma}
314	
315	
316	\leanok
317	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
318	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
320	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
321	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
322	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
323	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
324	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
325	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
326	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
327	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
328	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
329	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
330	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
331	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
332	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
333	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
334	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
335	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{proof}
339	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
340	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
341	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
342	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
343	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
344	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
345	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
346	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
347	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
348	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
349	\end{proof}
350	
351	\begin{lemma}\leanok
352	[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
353	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
354	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
355	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator,
356	        lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
357	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
358	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
359	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
360	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
361	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
362	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
363	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
364	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
365	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
366	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
367	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
368	  normalisation with denominators).}
369	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
370	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
371	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
372	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
373	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
374	  \[
375	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
376	  \]
377	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
378	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
379	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
380	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
381	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
382	\end{lemma}
383	
384	\begin{proof}
385	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
386	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
387	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
388	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
389	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
390	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
391	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
392	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
393	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
394	
395	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
396	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
397	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
398	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
399	  \(x \in \sigma\), a standard integrality-and-localization descent argument yields a
400	  non-zero \(a_x \in A\) such that \(a_x \cdot (1 \otimes x)\)
401	  is integral over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
402	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
403	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
404	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
405	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
406	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
407	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the proof discharges a whole generator
408	  at once using this integrality-descent step, folding that primitive over the coefficients
409	  internally.)
410	
411	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
412	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
413	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
414	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
415	
416	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
417	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
418	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
419	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
420	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
421	  \[
422	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
423	  \]
424	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
425	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
426	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
427	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
428	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
429	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
430	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
431	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
432	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
433	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
434	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
435	
436	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
437	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
438	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
439	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
440	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
441	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
442	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
443	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
444	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
445	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
446	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
447	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
448	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
449	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
450	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
451	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
452	
453	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
454	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
455	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
456	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
457	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
458	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
459	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
460	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
461	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
462	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
463	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
464	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
465	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
466	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
467	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
468	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
469	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
470	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
471	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
475	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
476	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
478	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
479	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
480	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
481	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
482	\end{lemma}
483	\begin{proof}
484	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
485	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
486	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
487	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
488	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
489	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
490	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
491	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
492	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
493	\end{proof}
494	
495	\begin{lemma}
496	
497	
498	\leanok
499	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
500	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
502	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
503	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
504	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
505	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
506	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
507	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
508	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
509	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
510	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
511	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
512	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
513	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
514	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
515	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
516	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
517	  \[
518	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
519	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
520	  \]
521	
522	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
523	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
524	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
525	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
526	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
527	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
528	\end{lemma}
529	
530	\begin{proof}
531	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
532	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
