533	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
534	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
535	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
536	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
537	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
538	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
539	
540	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
541	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
542	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
543	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
544	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
545	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
546	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
547	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
548	
549	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
550	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
551	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
552	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
553	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
554	\end{proof}
555	
556	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
557	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
558	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
559	  \mathlibok
560	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
561	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
562	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
563	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
564	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
565	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
566	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
567	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
568	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
569	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
570	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
571	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
572	\end{lemma}
573	
574	\begin{lemma}\leanok
575	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
576	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
578	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
579	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
580	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
581	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
582	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
583	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
584	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
585	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
586	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
587	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
588	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
589	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
590	\end{lemma}
591	
592	\begin{proof}
593	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
594	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
595	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
596	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
597	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
598	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
599	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
600	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
601	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
602	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
603	\end{proof}
604	
605	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
606	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
607	
608	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
609	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
610	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
611	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
612	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
613	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
614	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
615	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
616	
617	\begin{definition}\leanok
618	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
619	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
621	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
622	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
623	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
624	  \[
625	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
626	  \]
627	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
628	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
629	\end{definition}
630	
631	\begin{lemma}\leanok
632	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
633	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
634	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
635	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
636	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
637	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
641	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
642	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
643	\end{proof}
644	
645	\begin{definition}\leanok
646	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
647	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
648	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
649	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
650	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
651	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
652	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
653	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
654	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
655	\end{definition}
656	
657	\begin{lemma}\leanok
658	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
659	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
660	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
661	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
662	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
663	\end{lemma}
664	\begin{proof}
665	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
666	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
667	\end{proof}
668	
669	\begin{lemma}\leanok
670	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
671	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
673	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
674	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
675	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
676	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
677	\end{lemma}
678	\begin{proof}
679	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
680	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
681	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
682	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
683	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
684	\end{proof}
685	
686	\begin{lemma}\leanok
687	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
688	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
689	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
690	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
691	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
692	  transformed monomial is the radix value
693	  \[
694	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
695	  \]
696	\end{lemma}
697	\begin{proof}
698	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
699	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
700	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
701	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
702	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
703	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
704	\end{proof}
705	
706	\begin{lemma}\leanok
707	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
708	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
709	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
710	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
711	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
712	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
713	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
714	\end{lemma}
715	\begin{proof}
716	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
717	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
718	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
719	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
720	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
721	\end{proof}
722	
723	\begin{lemma}\leanok
724	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
725	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
726	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
727	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
728	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
729	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
730	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
731	\end{lemma}
732	\begin{proof}
733	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
734	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
735	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
736	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
737	\end{proof}
738	
739	\begin{lemma}\leanok
740	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
741	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
742	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
743	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
744	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
745	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
746	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
747	\end{lemma}
748	\begin{proof}
749	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
750	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
751	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
752	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
753	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
754	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
755	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
756	\end{proof}
757	
758	\begin{lemma}\leanok
759	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
760	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
761	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
762	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
763	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
764	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
765	\end{lemma}
766	\begin{proof}
767	  By induction on \(q\): the isomorphism is additive and multiplicative, and agrees with
768	  \(\varphi\) on constants and variables, so it commutes with \(\varphi\) on all polynomials.
769	\end{proof}
770	
771	\begin{lemma}\leanok
772	[Constant inclusion under the variable-splitting isomorphism]
773	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
774	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
775	  For \(s \in R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), the composite of the inclusion
776	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) (sending \(X_j \mapsto X_j\))
777	  with the isomorphism \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) sends \(s\)
778	  to the constant polynomial \(s \in (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\).
779	\end{lemma}
780	\begin{proof}
781	  By induction on \(s\): a constant and each variable \(X_j\) (\(j \ge 1\)) map to themselves,
782	  and the isomorphism is an algebra map, so it sends products and sums of such to constants in
783	  the outer polynomial ring.
784	\end{proof}
785	
786	\begin{lemma}\leanok
787	[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
788	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
789	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
790	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
791	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
792	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
793	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
794	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
795	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
796	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
797	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
798	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
799	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
800	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
801	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
802	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
803	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
804	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
805	\end{lemma}
806	
807	\begin{proof}
808	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
809	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
810	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
811	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
812	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
813	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
814	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
815	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
816	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
817	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
818	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
819	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
820	\end{proof}
821	
822	\begin{lemma}[L5b engine anchor — quotient by a monic univariate polynomial is finite]
823	  \label{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite}
824	  \lean{Polynomial.Monic.finite_quotient}
825	  \mathlibok
826	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
827	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in S[X]\) a monic polynomial. Then the quotient
828	  \(S[X]/(f)\) is a module-finite \(S\)-module (indeed free of rank \(\deg f\)).
829	\end{lemma}
830	
831	\begin{lemma}\leanok
832	[L5b.3 — single-variable elimination engine (shared)]
833	  \label{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
834	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_of_monic_lastVar}
835	  \uses{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
836	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
837	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
838	  % the division algorithm in one variable is the standard mechanism of the support drop.
839	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
840	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
841	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
842	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
843	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
844	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
845	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
846	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
847	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
848	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
849	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
850	\end{lemma}
851	
852	\begin{proof}
853	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
854	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
855	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
856	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
857	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
858	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
859	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
860	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
861	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
862	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
863	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
864	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
865	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
866	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
867	\end{proof}
868	
869	\begin{lemma}
870	
871	
872	\leanok
873	[L5b — torsion reindex onto fewer variables]
874	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
875	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_reindex}
876	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
877	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
878	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
879	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
880	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768).
881	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
882	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
883	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
884	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
885	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
886	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
887	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
888	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
889	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
890	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
891	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
892	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
893	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
894	\end{lemma}
895

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
5	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
6	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
7	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
8	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
9	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
10	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
11	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
12	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
13	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
14	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
15	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
16	of the trivial sheaf.
17	
18	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
19	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
20	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
21	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
22	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
23	
24	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
25	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
26	
27	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
28	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
29	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules that are not
30	available off the shelf and are therefore set up here. The first is the predicate
31	``locally free of rank \(d\)'', which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the
32	members of some open cover, isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\);
33	it is the condition cut out in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. The
34	second is a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of
35	such sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
36	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
37	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
38	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\).
39	
40	\begin{definition}
41	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
42	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
44	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{M}\) a sheaf of
45	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
46	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
47	  \(d\)} if there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}_{i}\) of \(X\) -- a family of
48	  opens with \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\) -- such that for each index \(i\) the
49	  restriction \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i}\) of \(\mathcal{M}\) to \(U_i\) is isomorphic,
50	  as a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules, to the trivial rank-\(d\) bundle
51	  \[
52	    \mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\,d}
53	      \;=\; \bigoplus_{k \in \{1,\dots,d\}} \mathcal{O}_{U_i},
54	  \]
55	  the free sheaf on the index set of size \(d\). This is the analogue, for the
56	  rank-\(d\) free model, of the ``open cover together with a local isomorphism''
57	  shape used to express quasi-coherence and local finite presentation: it
58	  asserts the existence of a trivialising cover on which \(\mathcal{M}\) acquires
59	  a basis of \(d\) sections. A sheaf that is locally free of rank \(d\) is in
60	  particular quasi-coherent and locally of finite presentation, and its fibre at
61	  every point is a \(d\)-dimensional vector space. This is the condition imposed
62	  on the member sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) in a family of rank-\(d\) quotients
63	  (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}); the universal sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)
64	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}) satisfies it tautologically, with the
65	  charts \(\{U^I\}\) as trivialising cover.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\begin{definition}
69	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
70	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
71	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
72	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
73	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
74	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
75	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
76	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
77	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
78	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
79	  charts. Suppose given:
80	  \begin{itemize}
81	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
82	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
83	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
84	      \[
85	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
86	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
87	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
88	      \]
89	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
90	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
91	  \end{itemize}
92	  subject to the \emph{module cocycle conditions} compatible with \(D\): the
93	  self-transition \(g_{ii}\) is the identity, and over each triple overlap the
94	  composite \(g_{jk} \circ g_{ij}\) agrees with \(g_{ik}\) once transported
95	  through the scheme-level triple transition of \(D\) (the multiplicative,
96	  \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle condition for the family
97	  \((g_{ij})\)). Out of this data the construction produces:
98	  \begin{enumerate}
99	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
100	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
101	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
102	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
103	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
104	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
105	  \end{enumerate}
106	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
107	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
108	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
109	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
110	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
111	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
112	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
113	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
114	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
115	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
116	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
117	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
118	
119	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
120	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
121	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
122	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
123	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
124	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
125	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
126	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
127	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
128	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
129	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
130	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
131	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
132	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
133	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
134	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
135	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
136	  built.
137	\end{definition}
138	
139	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
140	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
141	
142	\begin{definition}
143	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
144	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
145	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
146	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
147	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
148	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
149	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
150	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
151	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
152	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
153	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
154	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
155	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
157	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
158	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
159	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
160	  \[
161	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
162	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
163	  \]
164	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
165	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
166	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
167	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
168	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
169	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
170	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
171	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
172	\end{definition}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
176	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
177	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
178	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
179	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
180	    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
181	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
182	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
183	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
184	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
185	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
186	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
187	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
188	  %  by putting
189	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
190	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
191	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
192	  \[
193	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
194	  \]
195	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
196	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
197	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
198	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
199	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
200	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
201	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
202	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
203	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
204	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
205	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
206	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
207	\end{definition}
208	
209	\begin{definition}
210	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
211	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
213	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
214	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
215	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
216	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
217	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
218	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
219	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
220	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
221	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
222	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
223	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
224	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
225	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
226	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
227	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
228	  \[
229	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
230	  \]
231	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
232	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
233	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
234	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
235	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
236	  \[
237	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
238	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
239	  \]
240	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
241	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
242	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
243	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
244	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
245	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
246	\end{definition}
247	
248	\section{The functor of points}
249	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
250	
251	\begin{definition}
252	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
253	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
255	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
256	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
257	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
258	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
259	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
260	  % "represents the
261	  %  contravariant functor
262	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
263	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
264	  %  set of all equivalence classes
265	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
266	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
267	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
268	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
269	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
270	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
271	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
272	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
273	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
274	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
275	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
276	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
277	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
278	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
279	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
280	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
283	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
284	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
285	  \]
286	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
287	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
288	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
289	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
290	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
291	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
292	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
293	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
294	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
295	\end{definition}
296	
297	\section{The universal property}
298	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
299	
300	\begin{theorem}
301	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
302	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
304	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
305	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
306	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
307	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
308	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
309	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
311	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
312	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
313	  %  contravariant functor
314	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
315	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
316	  %  set of all equivalence classes
317	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
318	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
319	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
320	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
321	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
322	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
323	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
324	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
325	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
326	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
327	  \[
328	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
329	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
330	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
331	    \qquad
332	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
333	      \;\longmapsto\;
334	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
335	  \]
336	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
337	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
338	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
339	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
340	  universal family.
341	\end{theorem}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
345	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
346	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
347	    def:grassmannian_functor}
348	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
349	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
350	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
351	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
352	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
353	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
354	
355	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
356	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
357	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
358	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
359	  the composite
360	  \[
361	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
362	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
363	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
364	  \]
365	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
366	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
367	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
368	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
369	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
370	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
371	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
372	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
373	
374	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
375	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
376	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
377	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
378	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
379	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
380	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
381	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
382	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
383	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
384	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
385	
386	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
387	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
388	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
389	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
390	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
391	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
392	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
393	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
394	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
395	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
396	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
397	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
398	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
399	
400	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
401	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
402	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
403	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
404	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
405	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
406	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
407	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
408	
409	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
410	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
411	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
412	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
413	\end{proof}
414

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}\leanok
117	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
118	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
120	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
121	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
122	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
123	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
124	  % L2282-L2297).
125	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
126	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
127	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
128	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
129	  %  $$
130	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
131	  %  $$
132	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
133	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
134	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
135	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
136	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
137	  %  $$
138	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
139	  %  =
140	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
141	  %  $$"
142	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
143	  product).}
144	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
145	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
146	  \[
147	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
148	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
149	  \]
150	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
151	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
152	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
153	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
154	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
155	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
156	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
157	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
158	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
159	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
160	\end{definition}
161	
162	\begin{definition}\leanok
163	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
164	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
166	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
167	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
168	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
169	  % L4220-L4228).
170	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
171	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
172	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
173	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
174	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
175	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
176	  %  exactly $n$ times."
177	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
178	  powers).}
179	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
180	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
181	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
182	  on \(m\):
183	  \[
184	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
185	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
186	    \qquad
187	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
188	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
189	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
190	  \]
191	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
192	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
193	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
194	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
195	  invertible.
196	\end{definition}
197	
198	\begin{definition}\leanok
199	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
200	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
201	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
202	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
203	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
204	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
205	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
206	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
207	  \[
208	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
209	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
210	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
211	  \]
212	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
213	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
214	  \mathcal{F}\).
215	\end{definition}
216	
217	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
218	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
220	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
221	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
222	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
223	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
224	  the sheafification adjunction
225	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
226	  presheaves of modules, and write
227	  \[
228	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
229	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
230	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
231	  \]
232	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
233	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
234	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
235	  \[
236	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
237	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
238	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
239	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
240	  \]
241	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
242	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
243	\end{lemma}
244	
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
247	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
248	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
249	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
250	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
251	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
252	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
253	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
254	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
255	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
256	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
257	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
258	
259	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
260	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
261	  is the objectwise relative tensor
262	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
263	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
264	  maps
265	  \[
266	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
267	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
268	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
269	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
270	  \]
271	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
272	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
273	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
274	  pair
275	  \[
276	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
277	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
278	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
279	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
280	  \]
281	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
282	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
283	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
284	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
285	
286	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
287	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
288	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
289	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
290	  of \(a\)-images. Now the localization unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\) lies in
291	  \(J.W\), and the class \(J.W\) is \emph{monoidal} for the
292	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor: whiskering a local isomorphism by
293	  \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) again yields a local isomorphism. (This is the
294	  abelian monoidality of \(J.W\), itself proved through the fact that the
295	  internal hom into a sheaf is again a sheaf.) Consequently the two whiskered
296	  comparison maps
297	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) and
298	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
299	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) relating the two rows are
300	  isomorphisms.
301	
302	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
303	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
304	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
305	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
306	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
307	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
308	\end{proof}
309	
310	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
311	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
312	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
313	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
314	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
315	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
316	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
317	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
318	  \[
319	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
320	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
321	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
322	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
323	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
324	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
325	  \]
326	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
327	  arguments.
328	\end{corollary}
329	
330	\begin{proof}
331	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
332	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
333	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
334	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
335	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
336	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
337	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
338	  \[
339	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
340	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
341	    \qquad
342	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
343	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
344	  \]
345	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
346	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
347	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
348	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
349	  \[
350	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
351	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
352	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
353	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
354	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
355	  \]
356	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
357	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
358	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
359	  morphism
360	  \[
361	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
362	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
363	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
364	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
365	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
366	  \]
367	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
368	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
369	  \[
370	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
371	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
372	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
373	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
374	  \]
375	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
376	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
377	  composite
378	  \[
379	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
380	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
381	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
382	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
383	  \]
384	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
385	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
386	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
387	\end{proof}
388	
389	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
390	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
392	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
393	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
394	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
395	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
396	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
397	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
398	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
399	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
400	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
401	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
402	  %  (formulation omitted)."
403	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
404	  power isomorphisms).}
405	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
406	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
407	  \[
408	    \mu_{m,m'} :
409	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
410	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
411	  \]
412	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
413	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
414	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
415	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
416	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
417	\end{lemma}
418	
419	\begin{proof}
420	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
421	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
422	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
423	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
424	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
425	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
426	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
427	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
428	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
429	
430	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
431	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
432	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
433	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
434	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
435	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
436	  sheaf tensor products
437	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
438	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
439	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
440	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
441	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
442	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
443	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
444	  of the tensor product is never used.
445	
446	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
447	  \(m = 0\),
448	  \[
449	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
450	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
451	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
452	  \]
453	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
454	  recursive definition
455	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
456	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
457	  \[
458	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
459	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
460	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
461	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
462	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
463	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
464	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
465	  \]
466	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
467	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
468	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
469	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
470	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
471	  \[
472	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
473	      \mathcal{L}
474	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
475	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
476	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
477	  \]
478	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
479	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
480	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
481	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
482	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
483	
484	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
485	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
486	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
487	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
488	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
489	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
490	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
491	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
492	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
493	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
494	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
495	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
496	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
497	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
498	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
499	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
500	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
501	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
502	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
503	\end{proof}
504	
505	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
506	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
507	
508	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
509	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
510	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
511	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
512	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
513	global sections.
514	
515	\begin{definition}\leanok
516	[Section multiplication]
517	  \label{def:sectionMul}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
519	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
520	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
521	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
522	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
523	  \[
524	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
525	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
526	    \;\longrightarrow\;
527	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
528	  \]
529	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
530	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
531	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
532	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
533	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
534	  the sheafification unit
535	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
536	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
537	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
538	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
539	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
540	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
541	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
542	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
543	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
544	\end{definition}
545	
546	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
547	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
548	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
549	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
550	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
551	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
552	  the two composites
553	  \[
554	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
555	    \longrightarrow
556	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
557	    \cong
558	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
559	  \]
560	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
561	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
562	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
563	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
564	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
565	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
566	\end{lemma}
567	
568	\begin{proof}
569	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
570	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
571	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
572	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
573	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
574	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
575	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
576	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
577	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
578	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
579	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
580	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
581	  section multiplications.
582	\end{proof}
583	
584	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
585	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
586	
587	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
588	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
589	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
590	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
591	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
592	
593	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
594	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
595	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
596	  \mathlibok
597	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
598	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
599	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
600	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
601	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
602	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
603	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
604	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
605	  \(1 \in A_0\).
606	\end{lemma}
607	
608	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
609	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
610	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
611	  \mathlibok
612	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
613	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
614	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
615	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
616	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
617	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
618	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
619	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
620	  extension of the graded action.
621	\end{lemma}
622
