623	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
624	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
625	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
626	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
627	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
628	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
629	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
630	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
631	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
632	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
633	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
634	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
635	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
636	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
637	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
638	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
639	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
640	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
641	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
642	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
643	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
644	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
645	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
646	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
647	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
648	  multiplication is the composite
649	  \[
650	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
651	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
652	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
653	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
654	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
655	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
656	  \]
657	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
658	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
659	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
660	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
661	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
662	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
663	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
664	\end{lemma}
665	
666	\begin{proof}
667	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
668	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
669	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
670	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
671	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
672	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
673	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
674	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
675	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
676	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
677	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
678	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
679	  commutative-semiring structure.
680	\end{proof}
681	
682	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
683	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
684	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
685	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
686	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
687	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
688	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
689	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
690	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
692	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
693	  %  $$
694	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
695	  %  =
696	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
697	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
698	  %  $$
699	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
700	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
701	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
702	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
703	  \(X_s\). The family
704	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
705	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
706	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
707	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
708	  action
709	  \[
710	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
711	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
712	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
713	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
714	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
715	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
716	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
717	  \]
718	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
719	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
720	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
721	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
722	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
723	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
724	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
725	\end{lemma}
726	
727	\begin{proof}
728	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
729	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
730	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
731	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
732	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
733	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
734	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
735	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
736	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
737	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
738	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
739	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
740	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
741	\end{proof}

902	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
903	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
904	
905	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
906	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
907	
908	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
909	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
910	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
911	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
912	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
913	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
914	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
915	
916	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
917	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
918	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
919	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
920	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
921	  \(A_g\).
922	
923	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
924	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
925	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
926	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
927	
928	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
929	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
930	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
931	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
932	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
933	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
934	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
935	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
936	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
937	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
938	
939	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
940	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
941	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
942	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
943	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
944	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
945	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
946	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
947	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
948	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
949	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
950	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
951	
952	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
953	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
954	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
955	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
956	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
957	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
958	
959	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
960	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
961	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
962	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
963	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
964	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
965	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
966	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
967	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
968	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
969	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
970	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
971	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
972	
973	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
974	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
975	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
976	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
977	\end{proof}

979	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
980	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
981	
982	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
983	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
984	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
985	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
986	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
987	
988	\begin{lemma}\leanok
989	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
990	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
991	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
992	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
993	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
994	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
995	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
996	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
997	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
998	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
999	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1000	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1001	\end{lemma}
1002	\begin{proof}
1003	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1004	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1005	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1006	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1007	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1008	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1009	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1010	\end{proof}

1073	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1074	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1075	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1076	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1077	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1078	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1079	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1080	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1081	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1082	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1083	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1084	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1085	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).

1088	\begin{proof}
1089	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1090	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1091	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1092	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1093	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1094	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1095	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1096	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1097	
1098	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1099	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1100	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1101	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1102	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1103	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1104	
1105	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1106	  \[
1107	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1108	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1109	  \]
1110	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1111	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1112	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1113	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1114	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1115	
1116	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1117	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1118	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1119	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1120	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1121	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1122	\end{proof}

1152	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1153	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,

1156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1157	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1158	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1159	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1160	  corresponding polynomial ring.

1163	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1164	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.

1166	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1167	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1168	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1169	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1170	  \begin{enumerate}
1171	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1172	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1173	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1174	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1175	      \(P_d\)-modules
1176	      \[
1177	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1178	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1179	      \]
1180	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1181	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1182	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1183	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1184	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1185	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1186	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1187	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1188	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1189	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1190	      to \(A_g\).
1191	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1192	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1193	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1194	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1195	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1196	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1197	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1198	      over \(A_f\).
1199	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1200	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1201	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1202	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1203	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1204	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1205	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1206	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1207	      completes the induction.
1208	  \end{enumerate}
1209	\end{proof}
1210	
1211	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1212	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1213	
1214	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1215	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1216	
1217	\begin{lemma}
1218	
1219	
1220	
1221	\leanok
1222	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1223	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1224	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1225	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1226	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1227	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1228	\end{lemma}
1229	\begin{proof}
1230	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1231	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); free modules are flat.
1232	\end{proof}
1233	
1234	\begin{lemma}
1235	
1236	
1237	
1238	\leanok
1239	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1240	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1242	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1243	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a
1244	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1245	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1246	  is free over \(A_f\).
1247	\end{lemma}
1248	\begin{proof}
1249	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1250	  Since \(B\) is module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\),
1251	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1252	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1253	\end{proof}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open.
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1267	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1268	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1269	  \mathlibok
1270	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1271	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1272	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1273	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1274	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1275	\end{lemma}
1276	
1277	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1278	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1279	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1281	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1282	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1283	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1284	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1285	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1286	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1287	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1288	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1289	\end{lemma}
1290	
1291	\begin{proof}
1292	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1293	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1294	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1295	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1296	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1297	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1298	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1299	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1300	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1301	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1302	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1303	\end{proof}
1304	
1305	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1306	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]
1307	  \label{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1308	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1309	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1310	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1311	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof, gluing step
1312	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2107-L2109).
1313	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1314	  % "Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
1315	  %  Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}."
1316	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (gluing step,
1317	  Algebra Lemma \texttt{lemma-cover}).}
1318	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(X\), and
1319	  \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) is a finite
1320	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1321	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1322	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1323	  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{proof}
1327	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1328	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1329	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1330	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1331	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1332	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1333	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1334	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1335	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1336	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1337	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1338	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1339	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
1340	  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1341	  by hypothesis; the model-transfer lemma
1342	  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
1343	  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
1344	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
1345	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
1346	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).

1349	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict along a basic-open immersion]
1350	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1351	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1352	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
1353	  Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) is
1354	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Then for any open immersion
1355	  \(D(g) \hookrightarrow Y\) the restricted family — the images of those generators under
1356	  the section-restriction \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) — is again a finite
1357	  generating family of \(\F|_{D(g)}\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open
1358	  (a family that generates every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(D(g)\)),
1359	  and the index set, and hence finiteness, is unchanged.
1360	\end{lemma}
1361	
1362	\begin{proof}
1363	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1364	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1365	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the open immersion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow Y\) the
1366	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_{D(g)}\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in D(g)\) the stalk
1367	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1368	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1369	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I} \to \F|_{D(g)}\) is surjective on every stalk over \(D(g)\), so
1370	  \(\{s_i|_{D(g)}\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_{D(g)}\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1371	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved.
1372	\end{proof}
1373	
1374	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1375	[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1376	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1377	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1378	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1379	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1380	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1381	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1382	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1383	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1384	\end{lemma}
1385	
1386	\begin{proof}
1387	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1388	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1389	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1390	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1391	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1392	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1393	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1394	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1395	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1396	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1397	\end{proof}
1398	
1399	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]\leanok
1400	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1401	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1402	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1403	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1404	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1405	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1406	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1407	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1408	\end{lemma}
1409	
1410	\begin{proof}
1411	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1412	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1413	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1414	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1415	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1416	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1417	  finite generating family.
1418	\end{proof}
1419	
1420	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1 — finite type yields a finite cover of globally-generated affines]
1421	  \label{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1422	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1423	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1424	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2101-L2107).
1425	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1426	  % "Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This means
1427	  %  there exists an open covering of $X$ such that $\widetilde M$ restricted to
1428	  %  the members of this covering is globally generated by finitely many sections.
1429	  %  Thus there also exists a standard open covering
1430	  %  $X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
1431	  %  is generated by finitely many sections."
1432	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1433	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1434	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
1435	  cover of generated affines).}
1436	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules of finite type,
1437	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1438	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
1439	  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
1440	  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
1441	  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
1442	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).
1443	\end{lemma}
1444	
1445	\begin{proof}
1446	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1447	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1448	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
1449	  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering \(\{Y_\alpha\}\) on each
1450	  member of which \(\F\) carries a finite generating family of sections. The intersections
1451	  \(\{Y_\alpha \cap W\}\) form an open cover of the affine \(W\). By the finite standard
1452	  subcover refinement \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} there is a finite family
1453	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1454	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) and each basic open \(D(g_i)\) contained in some
1455	  \(Y_\alpha \cap W\) — in particular \(D(g_i) \subseteq W\) is affine, and \(D(g_i)\)
1456	  embeds by an open immersion into the member \(Y_\alpha\) on which \(\F\) is finitely
1457	  generated. By the restriction-of-generators lemma
1458	  \cref{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict} the finite generating family on \(Y_\alpha\)
1459	  restricts to a finite generating family of \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\), so \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1460	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Finally, by the free-epimorphism
1461	  reformulation \cref{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}, this is equivalent to the existence
1462	  of a finite index set \(I_{g_i}\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g_i)}\)-modules
1463	  \(\OO_{D(g_i)}^{\oplus I_{g_i}} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g_i)}\). That the \(D(g_i)\)
1464	  cover \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). This is
1465	  Stacks' passage from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated
1466	  affines.
1467	\end{proof}

1469	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]

1484	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1485	[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
1486	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1487	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1488	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1489	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1490	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1491	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — the implication
1492	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
1493	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1494	  % epi over an affine.
1495	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1496	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1497	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1498	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1499	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1500	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1501	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1502	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1503	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1504	  \(B\)-module map.

1506	
1507	\begin{proof}
1508	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1509	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1510	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1511	  affine).}
1512	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1513	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1514	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1515	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1516	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1517	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1518	
1519	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1520	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1521	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1522	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1523	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1524	  \[
1525	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1526	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1527	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1528	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1529	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1530	  \]
1531	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1532	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1533	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1534	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1535	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1536	
1537	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1538	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1539	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1540	\end{proof}

1543	[Half 2, seam 2$+$3 — a qcoh epimorphism with finite source sections has finite target sections]
1544	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1545	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1546	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi, lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}

1551	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1552	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."

1555	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1556	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Suppose the source has finite sections:
1557	  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V)\) is a finite \(B\)-module. Then \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
1558	  \(B\)-module.

1562	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi, lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}
1563	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB.}

1565	  \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) over the affine \(V\): the section functor yields a
1566	  surjective \(B\)-module map \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\).
1567	  The source \(\Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V)\) is a finite \(B\)-module by hypothesis. A finite
1568	  module surjecting onto \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) forces \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) to be finite, by
1569	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}. Hence \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
1570	  \(B\)-module.

1653	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1654	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1655	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1656	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1657	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1658	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1659	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1660	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1661	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1662	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1663	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1664	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1665	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1666	\end{lemma}
1667	
1668	\begin{proof}
1669	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1670	  Throughout, flatness is established by the standard locality criteria for module flatness
1671	  over a commutative ring, applied on the \emph{base} ring; the source cover \(\{W_j\}\)
1672	  enters only through the stalks of \(\F\), and is never treated as a covering family of any
1673	  base ring.
1674	
1675	  \emph{Step 1 — free \(\Rightarrow\) flat on each patch.} Each \((M_j)_f\) is free over
1676	  \(A_f\) by hypothesis, and a free module is flat. Hence each \((M_j)_f\) is flat over
1677	  \(A_f\).
1678	
1679	  \emph{Step 2 — pointwise flatness of \(\F\) over the base on \(p^{-1}(V)\).} Fix a point
1680	  \(x \in p^{-1}(V) = X_V\) and put \(s = p(x) \in V = D(f)\). The cover \(\{W_j\}\) of
1681	  \(p^{-1}(U_0) \supseteq p^{-1}(V)\) contains a patch \(W_j \ni x\). On \(W_j\),
1682	  quasi-coherence identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of
1683	  the finite \(B_j\)-module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) (finite by
1684	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}); consequently the sections of \(\F\) over a
1685	  basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W_j\) are the localization \((M_j)_g\)
1686	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}), and passing to the limit the stalk
1687	  \(\F_x\) is the localization of \(M_j\) at the prime of \(B_j\) corresponding to \(x\).
1688	  Because \(s \in D(f)\), the element \(f\) is invertible in the local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) and
1689	  hence acts invertibly on \(\F_x\); thus \(\F_x\) is equally a localization of the inverted
1690	  module \((M_j)_f\). The local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) is the localization of \(A_f\) at the prime
1691	  of \(s\) (a point of \(D(f) = \Spec A_f\)), and localization preserves flatness; since
1692	  \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\), the localized module \(\F_x\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,s}\).
1693	  Therefore \(\F\) is flat over the base at every point of \(p^{-1}(V)\): the stalk \(\F_x\)
1694	  is a flat \(\OO_{S,p(x)}\)-module for all \(x \in p^{-1}(V)\).
1695	
1696	  \emph{Step 3 — from pointwise flatness to \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).}
1697	  Let \(U \le V\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) be affine, and write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\),
1698	  \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), and \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); here \(N\) is a \(B\)-module and \(B\) an
1699	  \(R\)-algebra. We prove \(N\) flat over \(R\) by reducing first on the base, then on the
1700	  source.
1701	
1702	  \emph{Base reduction.} By the base-maximal locality criterion for flatness it suffices to
1703	  show, for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\), that the localization
1704	  \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R\); equivalently, that \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over
1705	  the local ring \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\), where \(x \in U \subseteq V = D(f)\) is the
1706	  point corresponding to \(\mathfrak p\). In particular \(\OO_{S,x}\) is a localization of
1707	  \(A_f\).
1708	
1709	  \emph{Source reduction.} Flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over the local base
1710	  \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\) is checked at the points of the source: it suffices that
1711	  for every point \(y \in W\) lying over a generization of \(x\) the stalk \(\F_y\) — the
1712	  localization of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) at the prime of \(B \otimes_R R_{\mathfrak p}\)
1713	  corresponding to \(y\) — is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\). Each such \(y\) lies
1714	  in \(p^{-1}(V)\), so by Step 2 \(\F_y\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\). As \(p(y)\) is a
1715	  generization of \(x\), the comparison \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,p(y)}\) is a localization,
1716	  hence flat, and transitivity of flatness upgrades ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\)''
1717	  to ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\)''. The passage from these stalk-flatness statements
1718	  to flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) records that module flatness
1719	  over a local base ring is detected at the points of the source affine.
1720	
1721	  Combining the two reductions, \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) for
1722	  every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\); hence \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
1723	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1724	\end{proof}
1725	
1726	
1727	\begin{theorem}
1728	
1729	
1730	
1731	\leanok
1732	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1733	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1734	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1735	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1736	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1737	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1738	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1739	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1740	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1741	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1742	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1743	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1744	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1745	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1746	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1747	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1748	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1749	
1750	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1751	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1752	\end{theorem}
1753	
1754	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1755	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1756	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1757	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1758	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1759	% by restricting to a non-empty
1760	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1761	\begin{proof}
1762	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1763	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1764	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1765	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1766	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1767	
1768	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1769	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1770	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1771	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1772	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1773	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1774	
1775	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1776	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1777	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1778	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1779	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1780	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1781	
1782	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1783	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1784	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1785	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1786	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1787	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1788	
1789	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1790	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1791	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1792	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1793	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1794	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1795	\end{proof}

11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
83	
84	\begin{lemma}\leanok
85	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
86	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
89	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
90	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
91	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
92	\end{lemma}
93	
94	\begin{proof}
95	
96	
97	
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
101	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
102	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
103	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
104	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
105	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
106	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
107	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
108	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
109	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
110	\end{proof}
111	
112	\begin{lemma}\leanok
113	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
114	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
116	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
117	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
118	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
119	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
120	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
121	  isomorphism.
122	\end{lemma}
123	
124	\begin{proof}
125	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
166	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
167	their proof sketches.
168	
169	\begin{lemma}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
173	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
176	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
178	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
179	  \[
180	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
181	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
182	  \]
183	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
184	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
185	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	
191	
192	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
193	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
194	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
195	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
196	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
197	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
198	\end{proof}
199	
200	\begin{lemma}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
204	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
207	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
208	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
209	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
210	  \[
211	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
212	    \;\cong\;
213	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
214	  \]
215	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
216	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
217	  of \(N\).
218	\end{lemma}
219	
220	\begin{proof}
221	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
222	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
223	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
224	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
225	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
226	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
227	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
228	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
229	  equation of
230	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
231	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
232	\end{proof}
233	
234	\begin{lemma}
235	
236	\leanok
237	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
238	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
240	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
241	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
242	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
245	    \;\cong\;
246	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
247	  \]
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
252	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
253	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
254	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
255	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
256	\end{proof}
257	
258	\begin{lemma}
259	
260	\leanok
261	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
262	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
264	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
265	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
266	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
267	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
268	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
269	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
270	  \[
271	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
272	    \;\cong\;
273	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
274	  \]
275	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
276	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
277	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
278	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
279	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
280	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
281	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
282	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
283	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
284	\end{lemma}
285	
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
288	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
289	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
290	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
291	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
292	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
293	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
294	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
295	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
296	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
297	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
298	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
299	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
300	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
301	
302	  \medskip
303	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
304	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
305	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
306	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
307	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
308	  \(U \mapsto
309	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
310	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
311	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
312	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
313	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
314	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
315	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
316	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
317	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
318	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
319	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
320	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
321	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
322	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
323	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
324	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
325	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
326	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
327	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
328	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
329	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
330	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
331	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
332	\end{proof}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	
336	\leanok
337	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
338	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
340	  Let \(M\) be an \(R'\)-module and \(b \in R'\). Then the structure-sheaf
341	  restriction map
342	  \[
343	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
344	  \]

354	  By the defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the tilde-localization map
355	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
